summary: Cantrill reflects on his nine-year journey at Joyent, which far exceeded his original seven-year career planning horizon despite the typically short lifespan of venture-funded startups. He describes how Joyent's idiosyncratic culture and forward-thinking team helped them spot trends like Node.js and containers early, though being ahead of the market proved both blessing and curse. The post announces his departure and celebrates the company's wild ride through a metaphor of a musical with an exhaustive cast of characters.
key_points: Startups operate on accelerated timescales where seven years feels like seventy for established companies
Being ahead of the market is as much curse as blessing
Joyent's story could only be told as a musical due to its dramatic twists
The 'ex-Joyeur' game became a tradition to honor those who shared the journey
money_quotes: Seven years into the future for a startup is like seventy for a more established company.
Being ahead of the market is as much of a curse as a blessing, and Joyent lived many different lives over the past nine years.
The story of Joyent could only be told as a musical – an observation so profoundly true that any Joyeur will chuckle to themselves as they are reminded of it.
After nine years, I will go from a Joyeur to an ex-Joyeur – from a player to a piece on the board.
As a technologist, I am finding myself as excited as ever by snow-capped mountains on a distant horizon.
key_insight: The startup journey is a theatrical experience where survival itself is an achievement, and the people who share that journey—whether they stay or go—become part of a shared story worth celebrating.
